does: This paper investigates vulnerabilities in the alignment training pipeline of large language models by demonstrating a poisoning attack that exploits the human feedback mechanism. The authors show how adversarially crafted user-supplied prompts can subtly corrupt the reward feedback signals that guide model alignment, causing targeted performance degradation associated with specific keywords while remaining inconspicuous to detection. They present two attack mechanisms: a selection-based approach that elicits toxic responses paradoxically scored as high-reward by the feedback model, and a generation-based approach using optimizable prefixes to manipulate reward signals. Crucially, the attacks succeed in black-box settings without explicit knowledge of target model internals, demonstrating that alignment training procedures introduce new security risks alongside their benefits.
connects: This work extends recent research on adversarial attacks against language models and the emerging field of alignment security. It builds on growing awareness that RLHF and human feedback mechanisms, while crucial for making models safer and more useful, introduce new attack surfaces that prior work had not thoroughly explored. The paper connects to literature on data poisoning in machine learning more broadly, and to concurrent research examining vulnerabilities in reward models and feedback systems. It responds to the assumption that alignment training primarily mitigates risks, showing instead that the training pipeline itself becomes a vector for adversarial manipulation. The black-box threat model extends earlier work on prompt injection and jailbreaking by showing systematic approaches to corrupt the training signal rather than just manipulating individual outputs.
enables: This research points toward several future directions for both attack and defense. It opens investigation into how robustness of reward models directly impacts LLM safety, motivating work on certified defenses and anomaly detection in alignment training data. The findings enable exploration of poisoning-resistant training procedures, such as robust reward modeling, adversarial training for feedback systems, and methods for detecting malicious user inputs during training. More broadly, it establishes that feedback-based alignment requires careful curation and validation protocols, potentially spurring development of techniques to validate training data integrity. The work also suggests examining similar vulnerabilities in other feedback-driven learning systems and points toward the need for formal frameworks characterizing the security-safety tradeoffs inherent in alignment procedures.
